content.text: is a structured convention that defines how a development team — or a solo developer — uses Git's branching, committing, merging, and pull-request features to manage changes to a codebase over time. Git itself is a distributed version control system: every contributor holds a full copy of the repository's history, and changes flow between copies via pushes and pulls. A workflow is not a built-in Git feature but a social and technical agreement that answers three questions: where does stable, deployable code live, how does new work enter that place, and how are changes reviewed before they arrive?

In agentic AI projects, version control takes on an extra dimension beyond standard software engineering. You are not only tracking application code but also prompt templates, agent configuration files, tool definitions, evaluation datasets, and experiment results. A subtle change to a system prompt can silently shift an agent's behavior across thousands of interactions. Without a disciplined workflow you cannot answer the question "what changed between the version that worked and the version that does not." Git workflows give every change a permanent, inspectable identity — a SHA-1 commit hash — and a structured path through review before it reaches production.

Teams that write code without a shared branching convention converge on predictable failure modes: changes from two contributors overwrite each other, the main branch breaks because someone pushed untested code directly, hotfixes get entangled with long-running features, and no one can reconstruct which combination of changes caused a production incident. These problems compound as team size grows because the probability of simultaneous conflicting edits increases with the number of active contributors.

For AI agent systems the stakes are especially high. A deployed agent may have already sent emails, created database records, or triggered downstream workflows before a regression is detected. The ability to identify the exact commit that introduced a regression and restore the previous version within minutes is the difference between a contained incident and an extended outage. Additionally, compliance requirements in many industries demand an audit trail of what software was running at what time, and Git provides exactly that: an immutable, inspectable history.

. Multiple engineers can work on separate features simultaneously, each isolated in their own branch, without interfering with one another. The integration step — the pull request — is where work is evaluated before it joins the shared codebase, not after.
Each feature, fix, or experiment lives on its own branch. Developers can work simultaneously without stepping on each other's changes.
Every commit records who made what change, when, and why. This history is permanent, searchable, and can be used to blame, bisect, or restore any prior state.
Feature branches are free to create and cheap to discard. A risky refactor on a branch leaves the main branch completely unaffected until the experiment succeeds.
Structured code review via pull requests.
Changes are reviewed before merging, catching bugs, spreading knowledge, and enforcing team standards.
Pull requests trigger continuous integration pipelines — tests, linting, security scans, evaluation suites — so problems surface before they reach production.
If a release introduces a regression, you can revert a specific commit or redeploy the previous tagged version in seconds.
In agentic AI systems, tracking prompt templates and agent configs alongside code means you always know the complete state of the system at any point in time.
Git's underlying model — commits, trees, refs, directed acyclic graphs — is not immediately obvious. Concepts like rebasing, cherry-picking, and resolving merge conflicts require practice.
Process overhead for small or solo projects.
Opening a pull request, waiting for CI, and having someone review a five-line change is friction. For personal scripts or solo exploratory work the overhead exceeds the benefit.
When two branches modify the same lines of the same file, Git cannot automatically decide which version is correct. A human must read both changes and decide.
Binary and large files are poorly served.
Git stores every version of every file in full. A large model weight file checked in twice doubles the repository size permanently. Git LFS mitigates this but adds operational complexity.
Workflows that encourage many long-lived branches (like Gitflow) can become hard to manage as the number of active feature, release, and hotfix tracks grows.
Not a substitute for experiment tracking.
changes. For tracking model accuracy across hundreds of training runs, tools like MLflow or Weights and Biases are better suited than Git branches.
Git stores content as a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of immutable objects. The four object types are:
(directory listing that maps filenames to blobs or other trees),
(a pointer to a root tree plus parent commit hashes, author metadata, and a message), and

(a named pointer to a commit with an optional GPG signature). Every object is identified by the SHA-1 hash of its contents, which means two identical files always share a hash (automatic deduplication), and any bit-level change produces a completely different hash (tamper-evident history).

The commit hash is computed as the SHA-1 of a serialized record that includes the tree hash, the parent commit hash(es), the author's name and email, the timestamp, and the message. Changing any of these inputs — including the timestamp — produces a completely different hash. Formally:
in Git is nothing more than a named pointer — a text file inside

— that holds a commit SHA. When you commit on a branch, Git creates a new commit object with the old commit as its parent, then updates the branch pointer to point at the new commit. This makes branch creation and switching essentially instantaneous — there are no copies of files involved, only pointer updates.
tracks which branch (or commit) is currently checked out. When
points to a branch name (the normal case), new commits advance both
and the branch pointer together. When
points directly to a SHA instead of a branch name, Git calls this a "detached HEAD" state.
When integrating changes from one branch into another, Git offers two primary strategies. A
creates a new "merge commit" with two parents — one from each branch — and preserves the full divergent history of both lines of development. A

replays the commits from one branch on top of the tip of another, rewriting their SHA hashes to produce a linear history without a merge commit. Both strategies produce equivalent code states but tell different stories in the log: merge preserves the true parallel history; rebase produces a cleaner, easier-to-read linear sequence.

The three-way merge algorithm compares two branch tips against their lowest common ancestor (the merge base). If only one side changed a given region of a file, Git accepts that change automatically. If both sides changed the same region differently, Git reports a merge conflict for manual resolution.
, the simplest production-ready workflow. It uses one permanent long-lived branch (
) and short-lived feature branches that are opened as pull requests, reviewed, and merged after CI passes.

attrs.alt: GitHub Flow diagram showing main branch, two feature branches (feature/tool-calling and feature/prompt-cache), pull request badges, merge commits, and a Deploy step

content.text: The key invariant in GitHub Flow is that
is always deployable. Every commit on

has passed automated tests and at least one peer review. Feature branches exist only for the duration of active work — ideally days, never weeks. When a pull request merges, the feature branch is deleted, keeping the branch namespace clean.
Create a branch from an up-to-date
Make one or more focused, atomic commits on that branch.
Push the branch to the remote and open a pull request targeting
CI runs automatically: unit tests, linting, integration tests, evaluation suite.
One or more reviewers inspect the diff and either approve or request changes.
After approval and green CI, the branch is merged (or squash-merged) into
The deployment pipeline deploys the new
The feature branch is deleted to maintain a clean namespace.
Two or more people work on the same codebase — even two developers in different time zones need branch isolation.
You deploy to a production environment and need a clear boundary between in-progress work and live code.
You maintain prompt templates, agent configs, or evaluation datasets that must stay synchronized with the code that uses them.
You need an audit trail for regulatory compliance, incident investigation, or reproducibility of experiment results.
You run automated tests or checks that should gate deployment.
When a simpler approach may suffice
A true solo throwaway script with no deployment: version control is still beneficial, but pull requests add no value.
Early exploratory Jupyter notebooks where you are trying many directions simultaneously: use Git for checkpointing, but strict branching adds friction until an approach stabilizes.

Model training runs: Git tracks code changes, but run metadata — hyperparameters, metrics, artifact paths — belongs in a dedicated experiment tracker like MLflow or Weights and Biases, not Git branches.

# Initialize a new repository
git init agentic-assistant
cd agentic-assistant

# Configure your identity (once per machine, stored in ~/.gitconfig)
git config --global user.name "Jane Dev"
git config --global user.email "jane@example.com"

# Create a .gitignore before the first commit
cat >> .gitignore << 'EOF'
.env
.env.local
*.key
*.pem
__pycache__/
.venv/
results/
*.pyc
EOF

# Create an initial commit
echo "# Agentic Assistant" > README.md
git add README.md .gitignore
git commit -m "chore: initial commit with .gitignore"

# Add a remote (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, etc.)
git remote add origin https://github.com/example/agentic-assistant.git
git push -u origin main

# 1. Always start from an up-to-date main
git checkout main
git pull origin main

# 2. Create a descriptively named feature branch
git checkout -b feature/anthropic-tool-calling

# 3. Work — make focused, atomic commits as you go
#    (after editing src/agent.py and tests/test_agent.py)
git add src/agent.py tests/test_agent.py
git commit -m "feat(agent): add tool_choice parameter support

Allow callers to force a specific tool via the tool_choice parameter
when invoking the agent. Follows the Anthropic API convention:
  {'type': 'tool', 'name': 'my_tool'}

Closes #42"

# 4. Push the branch and open a pull request
git push -u origin feature/anthropic-tool-calling
#    GitHub prints the PR URL in the push output

# 5. After approval and green CI, merge on GitHub
#    Then locally:
git checkout main
git pull origin main          # pull the merged result
git branch -d feature/anthropic-tool-calling  # clean up

# 6. Tag a release when ready
git tag -a v1.2.0 -m "Release v1.2.0: tool_choice support"
git push origin v1.2.0

# Scenario: two branches both edited prompts/system.txt
git checkout feature/new-system-prompt
git merge main
# Git reports: CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in prompts/system.txt

# The file now contains conflict markers — open it in your editor:
#
# <<<<<<< HEAD  (your branch version)
# You are a helpful assistant that answers questions about ...
# =======       (separator)
# You are an expert AI assistant specialized in ...
# >>>>>>> main  (incoming version from main)
#
# Edit the file to the correct combined resolution, removing markers:
# (e.g.) You are an expert AI assistant that answers questions about ...

git add prompts/system.txt
git commit   # Git pre-fills a merge commit message — accept or edit it

# Alternatively, use a visual merge tool
git mergetool  # opens configured diff tool (VS Code, vimdiff, etc.)
Prompt templates are plain text files, so Git tracks them exactly like application code. Here is a minimal pattern for loading the currently committed prompt at runtime:

# src/prompts.py
from pathlib import Path

PROMPTS_DIR = Path(__file__).parent.parent / "prompts"


def load_prompt(name: str) -> str:
    """Load a prompt template from the prompts/ directory.

    Prompt files are versioned in Git alongside the code that uses them.
    Running `git log prompts/system.txt` shows the complete evolution of
    the system prompt with author, timestamp, and rationale for each change.
    """
    path = PROMPTS_DIR / f"{name}.txt"
    if not path.exists():
        raise FileNotFoundError(
            f"Prompt '{name}' not found. "
            f"Expected file at: {path}"
        )
    return path.read_text(encoding="utf-8").strip()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    system_prompt = load_prompt("system")
    user_prompt = load_prompt("user_greeting")
    print(f"System prompt: {len(system_prompt)} chars")
    print(f"User prompt:   {len(user_prompt)} chars")
When testing a prompt change against a benchmark, create a branch so the experiment is fully reproducible and comparable against
at any future point. The Git SHA is embedded in the results file, permanently linking a score to a specific codebase state:

# scripts/run_eval.py
"""Evaluate the agent against labeled test cases and record results.

Usage (from repo root):
    python scripts/run_eval.py

The branch name and commit SHA are embedded in the output filename so that
results can be correlated with Git history at any future point.
"""
import json
import subprocess
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path

import anthropic


def get_git_context() -> dict[str, str]:
    """Return the current branch name and HEAD commit SHA."""
    sha = subprocess.check_output(
        ["git", "rev-parse", "HEAD"], text=True
    ).strip()
    branch = subprocess.check_output(
        ["git", "rev-parse", "--abbrev-ref", "HEAD"], text=True
    ).strip()
    return {"sha": sha, "branch": branch}


def run_evaluation(
    prompts_dir: Path,
    client: anthropic.Anthropic,
) -> list[dict]:
    system = (prompts_dir / "system.txt").read_text().strip()
    cases = json.loads((prompts_dir / "eval_cases.json").read_text())
    results = []
    for case in cases:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=512,
            system=system,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": case["input"]}],
        )
        answer = response.content[0].text
        passed = case["expected"].lower() in answer.lower()
        results.append({
            "input": case["input"],
            "expected": case["expected"],
            "answer": answer,
            "passed": passed,
        })
    return results


def main() -> None:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    prompts_dir = Path("prompts")
    git_ctx = get_git_context()

    results = run_evaluation(prompts_dir, client)
    accuracy = sum(r["passed"] for r in results) / len(results)

    output = {
        "git": git_ctx,
        "timestamp": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        "accuracy": round(accuracy, 4),
        "total": len(results),
        "passed": sum(r["passed"] for r in results),
        "results": results,
    }

    safe_branch = git_ctx["branch"].replace("/", "_")
    out_path = Path("results") / f"{safe_branch}_{git_ctx['sha'][:8]}.json"
    out_path.parent.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
    out_path.write_text(json.dumps(output, indent=2))

    print(f"Accuracy : {accuracy:.1%}")
    print(f"Branch   : {git_ctx['branch']}")
    print(f"SHA      : {git_ctx['sha'][:8]}")
    print(f"Output   : {out_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Production AI agent with evaluation-gated deployments

A team of six engineers maintains an agentic customer support assistant that classifies tickets, retrieves knowledge base articles, and takes actions in a CRM system. The system consists of an orchestrator, a RAG pipeline backed by a vector database, tool functions that update CRM records and send emails, and an evaluation harness with 400 labeled test cases.
The team uses GitHub Flow with two additions: a

environment branch that mirrors production for QA, and mandatory evaluation score checks in CI. The rule is that a PR cannot merge if the evaluation accuracy on affected prompts or agent code drops more than one percentage point from

# .github/workflows/eval.yml
# Triggered on every PR that touches prompts, agent code, or tool definitions
name: Agent Evaluation

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - "prompts/**"
      - "src/agent/**"
      - "src/tools/**"
      - "tests/**"

jobs:
  evaluate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt

      - name: Run unit tests
        run: pytest tests/unit/ -q

      - name: Run agent evaluation
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: |
          python scripts/run_eval.py --output pr_eval.json

      - name: Check for accuracy regression
        run: |
          python scripts/check_regression.py \
            --baseline results/main_baseline.json \
            --current pr_eval.json \
            --max-drop 0.01  # fail if accuracy drops more than 1%

      - name: Upload evaluation artifact
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: eval-results-${{ github.sha }}
          path: pr_eval.json
Over 18 months the repository accumulated 2,400 commits across 380 merged pull requests. When an incident occurs, the team uses
to narrow the breaking commit from a range of hundreds down to a specific change in under ten minutes.

implements binary search over commit history: you mark one SHA as "good" and another as "bad," and Git automatically checks out the midpoint for you to test, halving the search space with each step. The number of steps required to find the breaking commit is at most:
For 256 commits between a known-good and known-bad state, bisect requires at most 8 test steps — a process that would take hours with manual inspection.
The most common beginner mistake is using
as a working directory rather than treating it as a stable deployment artifact.
pushing untested changes directly to the branch that deploys to production

# Do not do this for anything that will reach production
git checkout main
# ... edit files without testing ...
git add .
git commit -m "quick fix"
git push origin main  # bypasses review and CI entirely

# Do: use a branch, even for small fixes — it takes 90 seconds
git checkout -b fix/correct-tool-timeout
# ... make the fix and test it ...
git add src/tools.py
git commit -m "fix(tools): increase HTTP timeout from 5s to 30s

The downstream CRM API p99 latency is 18s under load.
A 5s timeout caused 12% of tool calls to fail during peak hours.
Fixes #89"
git push origin fix/correct-tool-timeout
# open a PR — CI catches issues before they reach users
Secrets committed to a repository are compromised immediately — period. Even if you delete the file in the next commit, the secret remains permanently in history and can be extracted with
. Rotating the key is the only safe remediation. Prevention requires multiple layers:

# Layer 1: .gitignore — add BEFORE creating sensitive files
cat >> .gitignore << 'EOF'
.env
.env.local
.env.*.local
*.key
*.pem
secrets/
credentials.json
EOF
git add .gitignore
git commit -m "chore: add .gitignore for secrets and credentials"

# Layer 2: pre-commit hook via gitleaks (see Tooling section)

# Layer 3: never hardcode credentials — always read from environment
# BAD:
#   client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key="sk-ant-...")
#
# GOOD:
import os
import anthropic
client = anthropic.Anthropic()  # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from env automatically

# If a secret IS committed, act immediately:
# 1. Revoke the exposed credential at the provider
# 2. Then clean history (requires force-pushing — coordinate with team):
git filter-repo --path secrets.txt --invert-paths
git push origin --force --all
A commit message of "fix bug" or "update stuff" provides zero debugging information six months later. The

# Bad — provides no context
git commit -m "fix"
git commit -m "update agent"
git commit -m "changes"
git commit -m "wip"

# Good — type, scope, summary, and optional body explaining why
git commit -m "fix(agent): handle empty tool_use block from Claude API

When Claude returns a tool_use content block with an empty input
dict (observed with claude-haiku-4-5 under high load), the tool
dispatcher was raising a KeyError on input['query'].

Now defaults to empty dict when input is None or missing.
Reproducer: tests/test_agent.py::test_empty_tool_input

Fixes #89"

# Common Conventional Commit types:
# feat     — new feature
# fix      — bug fix
# chore    — tooling, dependencies, config (no production code change)
# docs     — documentation only
# test     — adding or fixing tests
# refactor — code restructure, no behavior change
# perf     — performance improvement
A feature branch that lives for three weeks accumulates a large diff relative to

. The longer it lives, the harder the eventual merge becomes, and the more likely that CI on the branch no longer reflects what the merged result will look like. Prefer short-lived branches: if a feature is large, decompose it into a sequence of smaller PRs that each deliver a coherent, reviewable increment.
Each commit should do exactly one logical thing. A commit that fixes a bug, adds a feature, and reformats code cannot be independently reverted or cherry-picked.
Write commit messages that explain why, not just what.
— the problem being solved, the trade-off chosen, the ticket number.
makes automated changelog generation and semantic versioning possible, and makes
Target fewer than three days of life per branch. If a feature takes longer, decompose it into independent increments that each make the codebase better on their own.
Protect main with branch protection rules.
Require at least one approving review and passing CI before merging. Disallow direct pushes and force-pushes from everyone including administrators.
, OS metadata, and large artifact directories from day one to prevent accidental inclusion.
Use rebase to keep local branches clean.
keeps your local branch current without creating spurious merge commits in your feature branch history.
) create permanent, named snapshots that you can check out, deploy, or diff against at any future time.

Git is extremely fast for text-based repositories of typical size. Most operations — branching, committing, diffing, logging — complete in milliseconds locally because they only touch the local object store. A repository with hundreds of thousands of commits and thousands of files remains fast for daily operations because Git's DAG structure enables incremental computation. The effective complexity of common operations is:
: proportional to the number of changed files (
is the number of modified files)
: proportional to the number of commits traversed
: proportional to the number of changed lines
: constant time — just writes a new ref file

Git was designed for text source code. Binary files — model weights, audio, images, large datasets — cause two problems: Git cannot produce meaningful diffs (each version is stored in full as a blob), and the repository grows without bound because old blob objects are never deleted.
is the standard solution: it replaces large files in the repository with small text pointer files and stores the actual content on a dedicated LFS server:

# Install Git LFS (once per machine)
# macOS: brew install git-lfs
# Linux: apt install git-lfs
git lfs install

# Declare which file patterns should be stored via LFS
git lfs track "*.bin"              # PyTorch model weights
git lfs track "*.safetensors"      # Hugging Face model weights
git lfs track "data/eval/*.jsonl"  # large evaluation datasets
git lfs track "*.parquet"

# The .gitattributes file is created or updated — commit it
git add .gitattributes
git commit -m "chore: configure Git LFS for model weights and eval data"

# All subsequent adds of tracked patterns go through LFS automatically
git add models/agent_v2.safetensors
git commit -m "feat(models): add fine-tuned agent weights v2"
Very large repositories (millions of files, years of history) can make
slow in CI pipelines. Two mitigations address this.
) fetch only the most recent commit without history, making CI clones much faster at the cost of being unable to use
fetches only the subdirectory a specific CI job needs, reducing disk I/O in monorepo setups where different jobs own different directories.
— the most widely used platform. GitHub Actions for CI/CD, branch protection rules, CODEOWNERS, Dependabot for automated dependency updates, and GitHub Advanced Security for secret scanning.
— strong built-in CI/CD with GitLab CI; popular in enterprises and self-hosted environments. Built-in container registry and package registry.
— integrates tightly with the Atlassian ecosystem (Jira, Confluence). Popular in teams already using those tools.
framework runs automated quality checks before each commit, catching problems before they enter the repository:

# .pre-commit-config.yaml
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
    rev: v4.6.0
    hooks:
      - id: trailing-whitespace        # remove trailing whitespace
      - id: end-of-file-fixer          # ensure files end with a newline
      - id: detect-private-key         # block accidental key commits
      - id: check-json                 # validate JSON syntax
      - id: check-yaml                 # validate YAML syntax

  - repo: https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff-pre-commit
    rev: v0.5.0
    hooks:
      - id: ruff                       # Python linting (very fast)
        args: [--fix]
      - id: ruff-format                # Python formatting

  - repo: https://github.com/gitleaks/gitleaks
    rev: v8.18.3
    hooks:
      - id: gitleaks                   # scan for secrets and credentials

# Install the framework
pip install pre-commit

# Install the git hook (runs automatically on git commit after this)
pre-commit install

# Run once on all existing files to establish a clean baseline
pre-commit run --all-files
(Python/Node.js) enforce the Conventional Commits format and assist with writing messages interactively. These integrate with
to automatically determine the next semantic version number (major/minor/patch) and generate changelogs from commit history — no manual version bumping required.
The three most common Git branching strategies each make different trade-offs between release control and integration speed. Choose based on your team's deployment model and maturity:
SaaS products, agent APIs, teams with strong CI
Libraries, desktop apps, regulated software with planned releases
main only (very short branches or feature flags)
Elite DevOps teams; requires feature flags and comprehensive test coverage
Teams deploying to multiple named environments (staging, pre-prod, production)
Open-source projects; outside contributors without write access to the upstream repo

is the right starting point. It is simple enough for a two-person team and scales to dozens of engineers with proper branch protection and CI automation. Graduate to Trunk-Based Development only when your test suite and feature-flag infrastructure are mature enough to support multiple daily deployments safely.
Every production repository should have branch protection enabled on

. The minimum recommended set of rules: require at least one approving review before merge, require status checks (CI) to pass, disallow force pushes, disallow direct pushes from any user including administrators, and require branches to be up-to-date with
before merging. These rules prevent both accidental and intentional bypasses of the review and testing gates.
Git allows commits to be signed with a GPG or SSH key, cryptographically proving authorship. GitHub and GitLab display a Verified badge on signed commits and can be configured to

signing on protected branches. This prevents an attacker who briefly gains write access to a developer's environment from injecting malicious code without it being attributable to an unauthorized key.

# Configure Git to sign commits with your SSH key (simpler than GPG)
git config --global gpg.format ssh
git config --global user.signingkey "~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub"
git config --global commit.gpgsign true

# From now on, every commit is automatically signed
git commit -m "feat: signed commit"

# Verify the signature on the latest commit
git log --show-signature -1

# Verify a specific commit
git verify-commit abc1234
commits but can still be staged if a developer runs
before setting up the ignore. Use
as a pre-commit hook to scan for credential patterns before commits are created.
If a secret is committed, act immediately.
First revoke the exposed credential at the provider. Then clean the history using
and force-push (coordinate with all contributors to re-clone). Never rely on "deleting the commit" — the object exists in every clone that was already pulled.

GitHub Advanced Security and GitLab Ultimate include automatic scanning that alerts when known credential patterns (Anthropic API keys, AWS credentials, etc.) appear in commits. Enable this for any repository containing application code.
file can require review from a security team member for changes to
— directories where a malicious or careless change has outsized impact.

Git's SHA-based object store makes silent data corruption effectively impossible — a corrupted object produces a hash mismatch that Git detects and reports. The distributed nature of Git means every contributor's clone is a full backup; losing the central hosting server means losing the web interface, not the history. Run
periodically (in CI or as a cron job) to verify object integrity. Ensure your hosting provider takes regular backups of repository data and that you can restore to a point-in-time snapshot.
Think of a Git repository as a

branch is the official timeline — the one that defines reality for your users. A feature branch is like stepping into a parallel universe at a specific moment in time: you can do whatever you like in that universe without affecting the original. When your experiment succeeds, you
, collapsing the parallel universe back into the main timeline. When the experiment fails, you delete the branch — the parallel universe disappears without a trace on the main timeline.

A commit hash is the coordinates in that timeline — the precise address of a moment in history. Given any SHA, Git can reconstruct the complete state of every file in the project at that exact instant, regardless of how much time has passed or how many commits have come after. This is why

works: you provide two coordinate pairs ("this SHA was good, this SHA is bad"), and Git binary-searches through the timeline between them, checking out intermediate moments until it finds the exact instant the universe diverged from good to bad.
A Git workflow is a team convention, not a Git feature — it defines where stable code lives, how new work enters it, and how changes are reviewed before they arrive.

GitHub Flow (main plus short-lived feature branches plus pull requests) is the right default for most agentic AI projects — simple to learn, maps cleanly to continuous deployment, and scales from solo to dozens of engineers.
Every commit is identified by a cryptographic SHA-1 hash, making history immutable and tamper-evident — this is the foundation of auditability, rollback, and bisect.
Protect the main branch: require CI to pass and at least one peer review before any PR can merge, and disallow direct pushes from everyone including administrators.
Prompt templates, agent configurations, tool schemas, and evaluation datasets should live in Git alongside code — they are part of the system's observable behavior and must be reproducible.
Never commit secrets to a repository; even deleted secrets remain in history and must be treated as permanently compromised. Rotate the key and clean history with git filter-repo.
Keep branches short-lived — under three days is a practical target. Long-lived branches accumulate merge conflicts and diverge from main in ways that automated tests cannot detect.
Conventional Commits (feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor) make history searchable by humans and parseable by tools, enabling automated changelogs and semantic version bumping with no manual effort.
